text: This is a video of a patient that presented to the hospital with abdominal pain. He had a hiatus scan that was positive for acute cholecystitis. He was scheduled therefore for a cholecystectomy while he was in the hospital.
The surgical field reveals extensive omental adhesions and the duodenum tightly adherent to the gallbladder in the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the duodenum from the gallbladder, preparing to use scissors for further dissection.

The surgeon uses cold scissors to carefully perform sharp dissection, attempting to separate the duodenum from the gallbladder. The gallbladder's mucosal surface is visible, and the suction irrigator is employed to clear the field, revealing a stone inside the gallbladder.

The surgeon enlarges the incision in the gallbladder using hook electrocautery, facilitating the removal of a large stone from the gallbladder. The interior of the gallbladder is visible, aiding in orientation relative to the common bile duct.

The surgeon attempts to remove more of the duodenum from the gallbladder, focusing on excising as much of the gallbladder wall as possible. Extensive adhesions are visible, complicating the procedure.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to remove portions of the gallbladder wall, attempting to orient towards the common bile duct. The procedure is halted due to concerns about potential harm.

The surgeon switches from white mode to near infrared mode, revealing bile emerging from the cystic duct. Blunt dissection is used to remove more of the gallbladder wall from the cystic plate of the liver, although the ICG imaging does not provide useful guidance.
The surgeon encounters bleeding from the liver and applies a hemostatic agent, resembling snow, to the area while applying pressure to control the bleeding.
The camera focuses on the liver bed, highlighting the area where bleeding is occurring.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis and proceeds to close the cystic duct opening inside the gallbladder using two 5-0 PDS sutures, carefully avoiding deep sutures to prevent stricture of the common bile duct.

The camera is switched from white mode to near infrared mode, showing no further bile leak from the cystic duct. Due to extensive adhesions, an intraoperative EGD is performed, and the duodenum is submerged in saline for a leak test, revealing bubbling that indicates an entrotomy.
The surgeon identifies a small injury to the duodenum and repairs it using an absorbable barb suture in a running fashion, with the area of injury clearly visible.

The surgeon performs a repeat intraoperative EGD to check for leaks from the previously identified area, confirming no further leakage. The endoscope is advanced to the second part of the duodenum (D-II), showing normal appearance with no visible injuries.
A drain was placed which was removed on post-op day number one and he was started on diet on post-op day number one. Thank you.
